channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Download: Quantum computing for health, and why the world doesn’t recycle more nuclear waste
item.description:
Curator's Take: This MIT Technology Review piece highlights a significant $5 million prize challenge aimed at demonstrating quantum computing's practical value in healthcare applications, representing a crucial step toward proving quantum advantage in real-world scenarios beyond theoretical benchmarks. The Oxford-based quantum computer mentioned exemplifies the growing shift from laboratory curiosities to purpose-built systems targeting specific problem domains like drug discovery, molecular simulation, and medical optimization. Such prize competitions are particularly important for the quantum field right now, as they push researchers to move beyond NISQ-era proof-of-concepts toward solving actual problems that could justify the massive investments flowing into quantum technologies. The healthcare focus is especially strategic, given that quantum computers' potential advantages in molecular modeling and optimization could address some of medicine's most computationally intensive challenges.

This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. A $5 million prize awaits proof that quantum computers can solve health care problems In a laboratory on the outskirts of Oxford, a quantum computer built from atoms and light awaits…

Read original at MIT Technology Review
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Can quantum computers now solve health care problems? We’ll soon find out.
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial transition point where quantum computing moves from theoretical promise to real-world testing in healthcare applications. The UK's National Quantum Computing Centre represents a significant investment in bridging the gap between laboratory demonstrations and practical problem-solving, particularly in areas like drug discovery and medical optimization where quantum advantages could genuinely transform outcomes. What makes this development particularly noteworthy is the focus on neutral atom quantum computers, which offer unique advantages for certain types of calculations compared to the more commonly discussed superconducting systems. The emphasis on healthcare applications is strategic, as this sector presents some of the most compelling near-term use cases where quantum computing's ability to handle complex molecular interactions could deliver measurable benefits well before fault-tolerant quantum computers become widely available.

I’m standing in front of a quantum computer built out of atoms and light at the UK’s National Quantum Computing Centre on the outskirts of Oxford. On a laboratory table, a complex matrix of mirrors and lenses surrounds a Rubik’s Cube–size cell where 100 cesium atoms are suspended in grid formation by a carefully manipulated…

Read original at MIT Technology Review
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award
item.description:
Curator's Take: This recognition marks a historic moment where quantum cryptography pioneers Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard receive computing's highest honor, highlighting how quantum physics has fundamentally transformed information security. Their groundbreaking BB84 protocol, developed in 1984, established the theoretical foundation for quantum key distribution and proved that the laws of quantum mechanics could guarantee absolutely secure communication. The Turing Award acknowledgment signals that quantum information science has matured from exotic physics experiment to a cornerstone technology that major computing institutions recognize as revolutionary. This award validates quantum cryptography as not just a niche scientific pursuit, but as a field that will reshape how we think about digital security in an era of advancing quantum computers.

Duo bag award often described as the “Nobel Prize in Computing” The post Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights Denmark's strategic emergence as a major player in the global quantum race, showcasing how smaller nations can punch above their weight by aggressively attracting international talent and making targeted investments in quantum infrastructure. Denmark's approach offers a compelling case study for how countries can build quantum ecosystems from the ground up, leveraging their existing strengths in physics research and technology policy to compete with quantum superpowers like the US and China. The focus on international collaboration and talent acquisition reflects a broader trend where quantum leadership increasingly depends on creating attractive research environments rather than simply having the largest budgets. This development matters because it demonstrates that the quantum advantage may ultimately belong to countries that can most effectively combine resources, talent, and strategic vision, regardless of their size.

Ambition and international talent converge as Denmark scales up in quantum science The post International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology represents a major leap toward massively scalable quantum computers using neutral atoms, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits - far beyond today's largest quantum processors. The ability to trap record numbers of neutral atoms using a single metasurface could dramatically simplify the hardware architecture needed for large-scale quantum computing, addressing one of the field's most pressing challenges around scaling up qubit counts while maintaining control precision. Neutral atom platforms have emerged as strong contenders for fault-tolerant quantum computing due to their inherent identical properties and flexible connectivity, making this advance particularly significant for the race toward practical quantum advantage. This development positions neutral atom quantum computers as increasingly viable alternatives to superconducting and trapped ion systems, especially for applications requiring massive parallelism.

Technique boosts prospects for building quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits The post Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computers must overcome major technical hurdles before tackling quantum chemistry problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This study provides a much-needed reality check on one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications, offering concrete benchmarks for when quantum computers might actually outperform classical methods in molecular simulation. While quantum chemistry has long been heralded as a natural fit for quantum computers, the researchers' analysis of algorithmic requirements reveals that current and near-term quantum systems still lack the error correction and qubit counts needed to achieve meaningful quantum advantage. The work is particularly valuable because it moves beyond theoretical promises to establish specific technical thresholds, helping the field set more realistic expectations for when quantum computers will begin solving industrially relevant chemistry problems. This type of rigorous feasibility analysis is crucial as the quantum computing industry matures and seeks to identify which applications will deliver real-world value first.

Although the potential applications of quantum computing are widespread, a new feasibility study suggests quantum computers still face major hurdles in solving quantum chemistry problems. The study, published in Physical Review B, evaluates what criteria are needed for a quantum advantage in searching for the ground state energy of molecules. The researchers attempt this feat using two different algorithms with differing strengths and weaknesses.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IonQ | IonQ & University of Cambridge Launch Quantum Innovation Centre
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership marks a significant milestone for trapped-ion quantum computing, as IonQ's 256-qubit system represents one of the most powerful quantum computers to be deployed at a European research institution. The collaboration with Cambridge, a powerhouse in quantum research, creates an important bridge between cutting-edge academic theory and practical quantum hardware development. Beyond the impressive qubit count, this Innovation Centre positions the UK as a major player in the global quantum race, potentially accelerating breakthroughs in quantum algorithms, error correction, and applications across chemistry, materials science, and cryptography. The timing is particularly strategic as governments worldwide are investing heavily in quantum sovereignty, making this kind of academic-industry partnership crucial for maintaining competitive advantage.

IonQ partners with the University of Cambridge to deploy a 256-qubit quantum system and launch a new Innovation Centre to accelerate UK quantum research.

Read original at IonQ Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task
item.description:
Curator's Take: This discovery adds another entry to the growing catalog of quantum advantages, demonstrating that quantum systems can outperform classical computers in determining the parity of permutations - a fundamental mathematical problem involving whether an even or odd number of swaps are needed to rearrange a sequence. What makes this finding particularly intriguing is that it was "unexpected," suggesting researchers are still uncovering new domains where quantum mechanics provides computational benefits beyond the well-known applications like factoring and optimization. The work contributes to our theoretical understanding of quantum supremacy by identifying novel tasks where quantum computers have provable advantages, even if this specific application may not immediately translate to practical quantum algorithms. Such discoveries help map the full landscape of quantum computational power and may inspire new quantum algorithms for related mathematical problems.

The list of things that quantum features enable us to do just got a little longer The post Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in single-photon interferometry over 1.5 kilometers represents a significant step toward revolutionizing astronomical observations by enabling quantum-enhanced telescope arrays. The ability to preserve and synchronize individual photons across such distances using quantum memories could allow astronomers to link telescopes separated by vast distances, creating virtual instruments with unprecedented resolution capabilities. This development bridges quantum information science with practical astronomy applications, potentially opening new frontiers in observing distant celestial objects with clarity that traditional interferometry cannot achieve. The work demonstrates how quantum technologies are moving beyond computing applications to transform other scientific fields in unexpected ways.

Single-photon interferometry achieved over 1.5 km The post Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant milestone in the quest for topologically protected quantum computing, as researchers have successfully demonstrated read-out techniques for Majorana qubits - exotic quantum states that theoretically offer built-in error protection. Majorana qubits represent one of the most promising paths toward fault-tolerant quantum computers because their topological nature makes them inherently resistant to the environmental noise that plagues conventional qubits. While this read-out achievement brings us closer to realizing the theoretical advantages of Majorana-based quantum computing, the lingering scalability questions underscore the substantial engineering challenges that remain before these systems can compete with current superconducting and trapped-ion platforms. The work represents crucial progress in what many consider the "holy grail" approach to quantum computing, potentially offering a more stable foundation for large-scale quantum machines.

Mechanism could pave the way for more robust quantum computation, but questions remain over scalability The post Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial milestone in making quantum-secure communications practical for real-world deployment, as distributing quantum-encrypted keys over 100 kilometers brings us to the scale needed for protecting entire metropolitan areas. The achievement of device-independent quantum key distribution at this distance is particularly significant because it eliminates the need to trust the quantum hardware itself, addressing one of the most challenging security concerns in quantum cryptography. With cyber threats increasingly targeting critical infrastructure and sensitive communications, this citywide-scale quantum security network demonstrates that we're moving from laboratory demonstrations to deployable quantum internet infrastructure that could protect everything from financial transactions to government communications across major urban centers.

Device-independent quantum-encrypted keys distributed over 100 km The post Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This podcast features Bert de Jong discussing the Quantum Systems Accelerator, a major DOE-funded initiative that's working to bridge the gap between quantum research and practical computing applications. The QSA represents one of the largest coordinated efforts to move quantum technologies from laboratory demonstrations to real-world deployment, bringing together national labs, universities, and industry partners to tackle critical engineering challenges. De Jong's insights are particularly valuable given his role in overseeing efforts to develop quantum algorithms for scientific computing applications, an area where quantum advantage could emerge sooner than in other domains. This discussion offers listeners a rare glimpse into how federal research priorities are shaping the quantum computing ecosystem and the practical hurdles that must be overcome to achieve scalable quantum systems.

Bert de Jong of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab is our podcast guest The post Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating connection between quantum entanglement and computational complexity theory, suggesting that the amount of entanglement in a quantum system can serve as a measure of how difficult certain computational problems are to solve. The research bridges fundamental quantum physics with practical computer science, potentially offering new ways to classify problems based on their quantum resource requirements rather than just classical computational steps. While the summary is brief, this type of work is crucial for understanding the theoretical foundations of quantum advantage and could help identify which problems are best suited for quantum computers versus classical ones. The findings add another layer to our growing understanding of how quantum mechanics fundamentally changes the nature of computation itself.

Efficiency and hardness of a computation problem are revealed The post Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology represents a crucial step toward massively scalable neutral atom quantum computers, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits compared to today's hundreds of qubits. Neutral atom platforms have emerged as strong contenders in the quantum computing race due to their identical qubits and flexible connectivity, but scaling them to useful sizes has been a major engineering challenge. The ability to create super-sized atomic arrays using metasurfaces could give neutral atom systems a significant advantage over other quantum computing approaches, as they would offer both the qubit count needed for practical quantum algorithms and the pristine control required for fault-tolerant operations. This development positions neutral atom quantum computers as serious competitors to superconducting and trapped ion systems in the quest for quantum advantage at scale.

Technique lays the groundwork for neutral-atom quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits, say physicists The post Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between National Metrology Institutes marks a crucial step in establishing the measurement standards that will underpin the quantum technology industry's growth. As quantum sensors and other quantum devices move from research labs toward commercial applications, having internationally agreed-upon standards for calibrating and validating their performance becomes essential for trade, regulation, and consumer confidence. The involvement of NPL and other leading metrology organizations signals that the quantum industry is maturing beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations toward the standardization phase that typically precedes widespread adoption. This standardization effort could accelerate quantum technology deployment by ensuring devices from different manufacturers can be meaningfully compared and that quantum-enhanced measurements are trusted across borders and industries.

NPL has joined forces with other leading National Metrology Institutes to shape the international standards effort in quantum technologies The post Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A tiny light trap could unlock million qubit quantum computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough addresses one of quantum computing's most persistent scaling challenges: how to efficiently read out information from massive arrays of qubits without losing precious quantum data to noise and interference. Stanford's miniature optical cavities represent a clever engineering solution that could finally bridge the gap between today's experimental quantum processors with hundreds of qubits and the million-qubit systems needed for truly transformative applications like drug discovery and cryptography. The ability to simultaneously monitor hundreds of qubits through light collection marks a significant step toward the parallel readout architectures that large-scale quantum computers will require. While we're still years away from million-qubit machines, this optical approach could prove essential for the fault-tolerant quantum computers that will eventually revolutionize computing.

A new light-based breakthrough could help quantum computers finally scale up. Stanford researchers created miniature optical cavities that efficiently collect light from individual atoms, allowing many qubits to be read at once. The team has already demonstrated working arrays with dozens and even hundreds of cavities. The approach could eventually support massive quantum networks with millions of qubits.

Read original at Science Daily - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone from Silicon Quantum Computing represents a significant step toward making quantum computers more manufacturable using existing semiconductor infrastructure. While 11 qubits may seem modest compared to IBM's superconducting systems or trapped-ion competitors, silicon-based qubits offer compelling advantages including potential compatibility with conventional chip fabrication and longer coherence times at higher operating temperatures. The real promise lies in scalability - if silicon quantum processors can leverage the decades of miniaturization expertise from the classical computing industry, they could eventually become the most cost-effective path to large-scale quantum systems. This development strengthens the case for silicon as a dark horse candidate in the race for practical quantum computing platforms.

Australian spin-out Silicon Quantum Computing makes the case with a modality-leading 11-qubit processor The post Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers? appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough challenges one of quantum mechanics' fundamental principles - the no-cloning theorem - by demonstrating that encrypted quantum states can indeed be copied and distributed across multiple locations. The research represents a significant step toward building a practical quantum internet, where quantum information could be reliably transmitted and stored at network nodes without losing its quantum properties. By using encryption techniques that preserve the quantum state during cloning, this work opens new possibilities for quantum communication protocols and distributed quantum computing architectures. While the full technical details aren't provided in this brief summary, the ability to clone encrypted qubits could revolutionize how we think about quantum network infrastructure and data redundancy in future quantum systems.

“Elegant” result has implications for a quantum internet The post Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: Microsoft's continued investment in quantum developer tooling signals the company's commitment to building a comprehensive quantum ecosystem that extends beyond just hardware development. While the summary lacks specific details about the new tools, enhanced developer platforms are crucial for accelerating quantum algorithm development and making quantum programming more accessible to a broader community of researchers and engineers. This type of infrastructure investment often precedes major breakthroughs, as better tools enable more researchers to experiment with quantum applications across diverse fields from chemistry to optimization. The timing is particularly significant as the quantum computing field transitions from proof-of-concept demonstrations toward practical applications that require robust, user-friendly development environments.

Microsoft is committed to providing the tools that developers and scientists need to enable applications on quantum machines. The post Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform appeared first on Microsoft Azure Quantum Blog .

Read original at Microsoft Quantum Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating new approach to understanding one of quantum mechanics' most puzzling phenomena - how quantum states "collapse" during measurement. By treating wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow process rather than an instantaneous event, researchers are applying quantum state diffusion theory to characterize measurements through entropy production, potentially offering fresh insights into the fundamental mechanism that bridges quantum superposition and classical reality. This work could have significant implications for quantum sensing applications, where understanding the precise dynamics of measurement processes is crucial for optimizing detector sensitivity and minimizing decoherence. The research represents an important step toward resolving long-standing questions about the measurement problem in quantum mechanics while potentially enabling more sophisticated quantum sensing technologies.

Quantum state diffusion framework makes it possible to characterize quantum measurement in terms of entropy production The post Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
